19 SECTION 6A. Chapter 12C of the General Laws is hereby amended by striking out
20 section 21 and inserting in place thereof the following section:-
21 Section 21. (a) The center shall establish a continuing program of investigation and study
22 of the uninsured and underinsured in the commonwealth, including the health insurance needs of
23 the residents of the geographically isolated or rural areas of the commonwealth. Said continuing
24 investigation and study shall examine the overall impact of programs developed by the center
25 and the division of medical assistance on the uninsured, the underinsured and the role of
26 employers in assisting their employees in affording health insurance.
27 (b)(1) For the purposes of this subsection, “public health access program beneficiary”
28 shall mean a person who is an enrollee in a: (i) MassHealth program established in section 9A of
29 chapter 118E; or (ii) subsidized health care plan through the commonwealth health insurance
30 connector authority established in section 2 of chapter 176Q.
290

House Ways and Means Committee H.5500
31 (2) Annually, not later than December 1, the center shall submit a public health access
32 program beneficiary employer report to the clerks of the house of representatives and the senate,
33 the house and senate committees on ways and means and the joint committee on health care
34 financing. The center may consult with other relevant agencies and departments to prepare the
35 report. The report shall include data for the preceding fiscal year.
36 (3) The center shall utilize all available data, including, but not limited to, self-reported
37 data provided by individuals who apply for public health assistance, if applicable.
38 (4) The report shall include the following information about each employer of 50 or more
39 public health access program beneficiaries in the commonwealth: (i) the name and address of the
40 employer; (ii) the number of each type of public health access program beneficiaries who are
41 employees of the employer; (iii) the number of public health access program beneficiaries who
42 are spouses or dependents of employees of the employer; (iv) whether the employer offers any
43 employee health benefits; (v) the estimated cost to the commonwealth of providing public health
44 access program benefits; and (vi) an analysis aggregating public health access program
45 beneficiaries attributable to any single franchisor licensing 10 or more franchises in the
46 commonwealth.
47 (5) The report shall not include the names of, or other identifying information about, any
48 individual public health access program beneficiaries, and the report shall be subject to any
49 applicable federal and state laws.

112 “Excess investment income”, the amount in any calendar year where all realized gains

114 assumption set in the system’s most recent actuarial valuation. Such amount, in excess of said
115 investment return assumption, shall be based on the market rate of return during a calendar year
116 on the balance of total assets of the system at the close of business on December 31 of the prior
117 calendar year, less the amount required to be derived by the investment return assumption set in
118 said valuation. Not later than 90 days after the end of said calendar year, the relevant actuary of a
119 system shall certify the total excess investment income of the system.

House Ways and Means Committee H.5500
136 paragraph (a) shall not be deemed to have had an interruption of membership or service. Upon

488 Section 251. (a) As used in this section, the term “provider immunization brand choice”
489 shall mean that healthcare providers shall select specific manufacturer brands for their patients.
490 (b) Notwithstanding section 24N and any other general or special law to the contrary, the
491 department shall implement a provider immunization brand choice requirement as part of: (i) the
492 commonwealth’s universal immunization program under section 24I; (ii) routine childhood

303

House Ways and Means Committee H.5500
499 combination immunization and dosage form, as long as the immunization is licensed or
500 authorized for emergency use by the federal Food and Drug Administration and recommended
501 by the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization
502 Practices. The department shall not limit the ability of such health care providers to provide an
503 immunization by limiting the supply of immunizations purchased by the department. This
504 section shall not apply in the event of a shortage or delay in vaccine availability, disaster or
505 public health emergency, terrorist attack, hostile military or paramilitary action or extraordinary
506 law enforcement emergency.

508 vaccines, use the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention vaccine list established, and
509 periodically reviewed and revised, by the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

572 Section 17E. (a) As used in this section, the following words shall, unless the context
573 clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings:
574 “County correctional facility”, as defined in paragraph (f) of section 1 of chapter 125.
575 “Health care provider”, as defined in section 1 of chapter 111.
576 “HIV”, human immunodeficiency virus.
577 “HIV prevention drug”, any pre-exposure prophylaxis drug approved for the prevention
578 of HIV by the federal Food and Drug Administration.
579 (b) The superintendent of each state correctional facility and the administrator of each
580 county correctional facility shall ensure that within a reasonable time prior to release each inmate
581 of a state correctional facility and county correctional facility who has been committed to a term
582 of 30 days or more, and who is negative for HIV infection, shall be:
583 (i) provided information and counseling about HIV prevention drugs to prevent HIV
584 acquisition;
585 (ii) evaluated for the benefit of an HIV prevention drug, with the consent of the inmate;
586 (iii) provided with a supply of an HIV prevention drug prior to release, to an eligible
587 inmate with their consent; provided, that the supply of an HIV prevention drug shall, at the
588 inmate’s option, include administration immediately prior to release of the longest duration
589 injectable form of an HIV prevention drug, a 90-day supply of an oral HIV prevention drug, any
590 other clinically appropriate HIV prevention drug or a prescription for such supply to be filled
591 post-release; and
592 (iv) provided with information about requirements for medical monitoring after release to
593 ensure the safe and effective ongoing use of HIV prevention drugs; provided, that each state
594 correctional facility and county correctional facility shall develop and implement a plan to
595 connect each inmate receiving an HIV prevention drug to post-release medical and other services
596 to ensure ongoing HIV prevention therapy upon release.
597 (c) Any pre-release supply of an HIV prevention drug shall be provided at no cost to the
598 inmate.
599 (d) Each state correctional facility and county correctional facility evaluating an inmate
600 for an HIV prevention drug pursuant to subsection (b) shall ensure that information obtained for
601 such evaluation shall be kept confidential between the inmate and medical provider and shall not
602 be shared with security or administrative staff.

605 SECTION 39. Chapter 138 of the General Laws is hereby amended by striking out
606 section 19G, as appearing in the 2024 Official Edition, and inserting in place thereof the
607 following section:-
608 Section 19G. (a) For the purposes of this section, the following words shall, unless the
609 context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings:
610 “Brewery of origin”, any brewery at which a tenant brewer is duly licensed to
611 manufacture malt beverages other than at the host brewer’s premises.
612 “Distillery of origin”, any distillery at which a tenant distiller is duly licensed to
613 manufacture distilled spirits other than at the host distiller’s premises.
614 “Package”, a keg, cask, barrel, bottle, can or other container approved by the United
615 States Department of the Treasury Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau for malt
616 beverages or distilled spirits.
617 (b)(1) The commission may issue tenant brewer or tenant distiller licenses that authorize
618 the licensee to manufacture or package malt beverages or distilled spirits, respectively, on the
619 premises of a host brewery or host distillery to any individual applicant who is a resident and
620 citizen of the commonwealth or to a corporation, partnership or other entity which complies with
621 the requirements of section 26 and is a holder of a certificate of compliance issued pursuant to
622 section 18B. An applicant for a tenant brewer or tenant distiller license shall provide the
623 commission and the department of revenue with a true copy of the applicable alcoholic beverage
624 notice, permit or registration to manufacture, export and import as issued by the appropriate
625 licensing authority.
626 (2) To be eligible for a tenant brewer or tenant distiller license, the applicant shall: (i) be
627 licensed pursuant to section 18, section 19 or section 19C or a license holder outside the
628 commonwealth that is authorized to manufacture, export and import malt beverages or distilled
629 spirits, respectively; (ii) comply with any federal law regulating the manufacture, export or
630 import of malt beverages or distilled spirits, respectively, as identified by the commission in a
631 written guidance that shall be issued to each host brewer, tenant brewer, host distiller, tenant
632 distiller and wholesaler licensed pursuant to section 18; and (iii) shall have an approved
633 alternating proprietorship arrangement that allows the applicant to use the facilities, equipment
634 and employees of a host brewer or host distiller.
635 (3) A tenant brewer or tenant distiller may import such raw materials as are required
636 solely for the production and packaging of the malt beverages or distilled spirits, respectively,
637 including, without limitation, bulk malt beverages or bulk distilled spirits produced by the tenant
307

House Ways and Means Committee H.5500
638 brewer or tenant distiller, respectively, at its brewery or distillery of origin. The bulk malt
639 beverages imported by the tenant brewer and bulk distilled spirits imported by the tenant distiller
640 shall be packaged and shipped back to the tenant brewer’s brewery of origin or tenant distiller’s
641 distillery of origin, or to a wholesaler licensed pursuant to section 18 or to a license holder
642 outside the commonwealth authorized to import malt beverages or distilled spirits designated by
643 the tenant brewer or tenant distiller within 10 days after receipt by the host brewer or host
644 distiller, or, in the case of a tenant distiller who is licensed as a wholesaler under section 18 and
645 operates as a rectifier, to a retailer in the commonwealth licensed to sell distilled spirits at retail.
646 (4) Any product produced or packaged at the host brewer’s or host distiller’s premises
647 shall be removed from such premises within 10 days after the production or packaging process is
648 completed. The finished product shall be returned to the tenant brewer’s brewery of origin or
649 tenant distiller’s distillery of origin, or to a wholesaler licensed pursuant to section 18 or to a
650 license holder outside the commonwealth authorized to import malt beverages or distilled spirits
651 designated by the tenant brewer or tenant distiller, or in the case of a tenant distiller who is
652 licensed as a wholesaler under section 18 and operates as a rectifier, to a retailer in the
653 commonwealth licensed to sell distilled spirits at retail.
654 (c)(1) The commission shall require a tenant brewer and tenant distiller and a host brewer
655 and host distiller to maintain a record or log indicating which equipment is being used at any
656 time by the tenant brewer or tenant distiller in the production or packaging of malt beverages or
657 distilled spirits, respectively, and which employees are working on production or packaging of
658 the tenant brewer’s or tenant distiller’s product. Tenant brewers and tenant distillers shall be
659 subject to the same reporting requirements as host brewers and host distillers.
660 (2) A tenant brewer license and tenant distiller license issued pursuant to this section shall
661 not authorize the licensee to sell malt beverages or distilled spirits, respectively, to any person or
662 entity in the commonwealth other than a wholesaler licensed pursuant to section 18; provided,
663 however, that a tenant distiller who is licensed as a wholesaler under section 18 and operates as a
664 rectifier may sell to a retailer in the commonwealth licensed to sell distilled spirits at retail. A
665 tenant brewer licensee and tenant distiller licensee shall only be authorized to manufacture or
666 package malt beverages or distilled spirits, respectively, pursuant to this section.
667 (d) The annual fee for a license issued under this section shall be $1,000.

House Ways and Means Committee H.5500
886 SECTION 51A. (a) There is hereby established a special commission to study and report
887 on the appropriate level and structure of dental benefits provided by MassHealth and the health
888 safety net. The commission shall evaluate whether the MassHealth and the health safety net
889 dental benefits are clinically appropriate, financially sustainable and sufficient to ensure access
890 to medically necessary dental services.
891 (b) The commission shall consist of the following 17 members: the secretary of health
892 and human services or a designee, who shall serve as chair; the undersecretary of MassHealth or
893 a designee; the commissioner of public health or a designee; the chairs of the joint committee on
894 health care financing; the chairs of the joint committee on public health; 1 member appointed by
895 the minority leader of the house of representatives; 1 member appointed by the minority leader
896 of the senate; 1 member who is a representative of Massachusetts Dental Society; 1 member who
897 is a representative of Health Care for All, Inc.; 1 member who is a representative of The
898 Massachusetts League of Community Health Centers, Inc.; 1 member who is a representative of
899 the Massachusetts Health and Hospital Association, Inc.; and 4 members to be appointed by the
900 governor, 1 of whom shall be a representative of an oral health advocacy organization and 3 of
901 whom shall be dental providers that participate in MassHealth.
902 (c) The commission shall: (i) assess the current dental benefit structure, including
903 covered services and utilization among adult populations; (ii) evaluate alternative benefit
904 designs, including, but not limited to, dollar limits, defined service packages or other models and
905 their clinical appropriateness; (iii) examine the impact of benefit structures on patient outcomes,
906 including access to preventive, restorative and emergency services, oral health status and unmet
907 need; (iv) assess impacts on providers and other health resources, including participation in
908 MassHealth, financial viability, administrative burden, capacity and emergency care; (v) analyze
909 geographic variation in access to care and identify areas with provider shortages; (vi) evaluate
910 how benefit design influences provider enrollment and participation, including barriers and
911 opportunities; (vii) consider the needs of underserved populations and assess how benefit design
912 affects disparities in access and outcomes; and (viii) review best practices from other state
913 Medicaid programs and relevant national standards.
914 (d) Not later than January 1, 2027, the commission shall file its report and
915 recommendations with the clerks of the house of representatives and the senate, the house and
916 senate committees on ways and means, the joint committee on health care financing and the joint
917 committee on public health. The commission’s recommendations shall include, but shall not be
918 limited to, the appropriate level and structure of benefits, including changes to covered services,
919 limits or reimbursement based on the study and review of benefits pursuant to subsection (c).
